close_reason: Reordered openArticle() to navigate (window.location) before flushMarkReadQueue(). The API call still fires during navigation but the DOM-modifying .then() callback is harmless since the page is being replaced. This matches the original pre-ES-modules behavior.
description:
**Problem:**
With Hide Read Articles enabled, clicking an article to read it causes a visible flash: the article card gets the `.read` class styling, then gets hidden (`display: none`), and only then does the page navigate to `/article/{id}`. The user sees the card disappear before the article page loads.

**Expected behavior:**
Clicking an article should navigate immediately. The read status should be synced in the background without any visible DOM changes on the current page.

**Root cause:**
`openArticle()` in `article-actions.js` calls three things in sequence:
1. `markReadSilent(id)` — adds `.read` class to the card (visible styling change)
2. `flushMarkReadQueue()` — fires `api('POST', '/api/articles/batch-read')` (async, non-blocking)
3. `window.location = '/article/{id}'` — starts navigation

The API call from step 2 resolves fast (local server), its `.then()` calls `updateCounts()`, which calls `applyUserPreferences()`, which runs `applyHideReadArticles()` — setting `display: none` on all `.article-card.read` elements. This DOM mutation is visible because navigation has not yet replaced the page.

**Regression history:**
The original implementation (commit ff510f3) only called `markReadSilent(id)` which queued a 250ms debounced flush. Navigation happened before the flush fired, so the user never saw the card hidden. The explicit `flushMarkReadQueue()` call was added during ES modules extraction (commit 5291257), which made the API call fire immediately and its completion side-effects race with navigation.

**Fix options:**
1. **Navigate first, flush after** — Set `window.location` before calling `flushMarkReadQueue()`. The API call will still fire; the browser sends it even during navigation. The `.then()` callback modifying the DOM is harmless since the page is being replaced.
2. **Use `navigator.sendBeacon()` / `keepalive` fetch** — Fire the batch-read API call with `keepalive: true` so it survives navigation, and navigate immediately without waiting.
3. **Skip DOM updates in flushMarkReadQueue when navigating** — Add a guard so `updateCounts()` / `applyUserPreferences()` are not called when navigation is pending.

Option 1 is the simplest and matches the original behavior. The key change is reordering `openArticle()` to:
```js
export function openArticle(id) {
    markReadSilent(id);
    window.location = '/article/{id}';
    flushMarkReadQueue();
}
```
title: Fix article card visibly hiding before navigation when Hide Read Articles is enabled

When the flag is true (set by showHiddenArticles or search), applyHideReadArticles is skipped so read articles remain visible. description:
## Bug
When viewing a feed (e.g., feed/586 — Hacker News: Best, 412 articles all read), read articles don't appear even when using 'show all articles' or search.

## Root Cause
Two interacting issues:

1. **showHiddenArticles re-hides what it just fetched**: `showHiddenArticles()` sets `showingHiddenArticles = true`, then calls `renderArticles()`, which calls `applyUserPreferences()` → `applyHideReadArticles('hide')`. This re-hides all read articles immediately after rendering them. The `showingHiddenArticles` flag is never checked by `applyHideReadArticles`.

2. **Search results also hidden**: `search.js` calls `renderArticles()` for search results, which also triggers `applyUserPreferences()`, hiding any read articles in the results. Search should show all matching articles regardless of the hideRead setting.

## Fix
`applyUserPreferences()` (or `applyHideReadArticles()`) should check `getShowingHiddenArticles()` and skip hiding when the user has explicitly asked to see all articles. Similarly, search results should bypass the hideRead filter.

## Affected files
- `srv/static/modules/articles.js` — `applyUserPreferences`, `showHiddenArticles`, `renderArticles`
- `srv/static/modules/settings.js` — `applyHideReadArticles`
- `srv/static/modules/search.js` — search rendering
title: Read articles hidden even with 'show all' enabled on feed pages

## feedreader-7dq.25: Refactor _isStandalone
Key insight: vi.spyOn(module, 'fn').mockReturnValue() does NOT work for ESM internal calls — the internal code still calls the original function, not the spy. The correct approach is to mock the underlying browser API (window.matchMedia) that the function calls. Created a mockStandaloneMode() helper that mocks matchMedia to return {matches: true}.

## feedreader-7dq.3: Audit articles.test.js
Found 37 gaps, all fixed directly without a sub-epic. Main weakness patterns: (1) buildArticleCardHtml had many untested ternary branches (no-url, author, summary/content combos, image placeholder, sort-time fallback), (2) showHiddenArticles error path (showToast) was untested, (3) renderArticles didn't verify aria-busy, pagination state, or empty-list button visibility, (4) processEmbeds had 4 untested branches. Note: toast.js needed to be added as a vi.mock for error path testing.
## feedreader-7dq.4: Audit counts.test.js
Added 11 tests (14→25). Found mock state leakage: vi.restoreAllMocks() doesn't clear call counts on vi.fn() from vi.mock() — need vi.clearAllMocks() too. Key insight: the test file was missing a toast.js mock entirely, so the showToast error path was invisible. The alerts badge was completely untested. All aria-label attributes had zero coverage despite being accessibility-critical.

## feedreader-7dq.14: Audit queue.test.js
Added 4 tests (8→12). Module is tiny (39 lines). Notable: queueNext has no try/catch so API errors propagate — tested this explicitly. No sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.5: Audit drag-drop.test.js
Major gap: api.js and toast.js were not mocked at all, so the entire drop handler (both reorder and nesting paths) was untested. This is the most complex drag-and-drop module with async API calls, shift-key nesting, placeholder positioning, and cross-container sync. All branches now covered. The vi.waitFor() pattern works well for asserting on async drop handlers.

## feedreader-7dq.7: Audit feed-errors.test.js
Small module with good existing coverage. Main gap was no XSS escaping test despite escapeHtml being called. The banner-exists-outside-articles-view path was also untested. No complexity warranting a sub-epic.
## feedreader-7dq.16: Audit scraper-page.test.js
Major gap pattern: private functions called via initScraperPage (checkAiStatus, initAiForm, initManualForm) were completely untested — they have complex async flows with success/error/finally paths. modal.js was unmocked so openModal/closeModal calls were invisible. The initScraperPageListeners describe block uses a pattern of calling initScraperPageListeners() once at the describe level since listeners accumulate on document — this works well but means delegation tests share state.

## feedreader-7dq.17: Audit search.test.js
toast.js was imported by the source but not mocked in tests, so all error paths that call showToast were invisible. The setShowingHiddenArticles mock was imported in the mock declaration but never imported in test code for assertions. Key pattern: _resetSearchState calls setShowingHiddenArticles(false) so tests that check it wasn't called need vi.clearAllMocks() after beforeEach.
## feedreader-7dq.24: Audit views.test.js
Added 7 tests. Main gap: initView was completely untested, and migrateLegacyViewDefaults only tested the localMap loop, not the direct keys loop. Module is clean with good separation. No sub-epic needed.

## feedreader-7dq.20: Audit sidebar.test.js
Added 5 tests. Notable: the navigate-folder non-articles-page test in initSidebarListeners had zero assertions — just comments saying what should happen. The toggleFolderCollapse guard (if (catId)) for falsy values was untested. navigateFolder's null callback and missing folder-name fallback were both uncovered. No sub-epic needed.
## feedreader-7dq.8: Audit folders.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Main gap: initCategorySettingsPage was entirely untested (3 tests: success, error, no-ops). The delegation handler for unparent-category and delete-exclusion had zero coverage despite being registered in initFoldersPageListeners. openModal/closeModal from modal.js were never mocked so their calls were invisible. All success paths lacked location.reload() verification, meaning tests passed even if the reload never happened. Pattern: vi.mock('./modal.js') at module level is needed to verify modal lifecycle.

## feedreader-7dq.13: Audit pagination.test.js
Found 15 gaps, all fixed directly. Most critical: the entire error path (catch block with console.error + showToast) had zero coverage. The done-state transitions (empty articles → done, partial page → done, full page → not done) were unverified despite being core pagination logic. The include_read=1 param tied to getShowingHiddenArticles() was untested. Gotcha: loadMoreArticles has a finally block that calls checkScrollForMore(), which can recursively trigger another loadMoreArticles if the scroll position is near the bottom. Tests that don't want cascading loads must set document.body.offsetHeight to a large value.
## feedreader-7dq.18: Audit settings-page.test.js
Found 11 gaps, all fixed directly. Main weakness patterns: all default/fallback paths in initSettingsPage were untested (the || 'show', || 'card' fallbacks), runCleanup intermediate loading state was not verified, copyNewsletterAddress checkmark icon swap and null-button guard were untested. The loadNewsletterAddress call from initSettingsPage was not verified at all.

## feedreader-7dq.19: Audit settings.test.js
Found 7 gaps, all fixed directly. Key issue: toast.js was entirely unmocked so saveSetting's catch handler calling showToast was invisible to tests. Same pattern as counts.test.js — module-level vi.mock() needed plus vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach. Also added boundary tests for getSetting (empty string vs undefined) and edge cases for applyHideEmptyFeeds (empty/NaN badge text).
## feedreader-7dq.26: Over-mocking audit
Cross-cutting findings from auditing all 27 test files (792 tests total):

**Duplication hotspots**: toast.js (13 identical mocks), api.js (7 identical mocks), and ~100 inline fetch response shapes. These are the highest-value targets for shared helpers.

**Three fetch strategies coexist**: vi.mock(api.js) vs vi.spyOn(globalThis,fetch) vs window.fetch=vi.fn(). The window.fetch assignment is worst (no auto-restore). Should standardize.

**Stateful mock anti-pattern**: settings-page.test.js reimplements settings.js internals. Since settings.js is pure localStorage logic, the real module should be used.

**Mock-heavy orchestrator tests**: spa-nav.test.js needs 6 mocks for 4 tests, suggesting it should either use fewer mocks (more integration-style) or the module should be refactored.

**Go tests are clean**: httptest.NewServer pattern, minimal mocking. No action needed.

Created 5 follow-up tasks (.27-.31) for actionable improvements.
## feedreader-7dq.27 + feedreader-7dq.30: Shared mocks and real settings
Key pattern: Vitest __mocks__/ auto-mocking works well for modules with a simple, stable API surface. Place files in __mocks__/module.js alongside the real modules, then test files just call vi.mock("./module.js") without a factory.

For settings-page.test.js, using importOriginal to get real getSetting while mocking saveSetting was the right split — getSetting is pure (reads window.__settings), saveSetting has side effects (fetch). Tests became simpler and more realistic.

Gotcha: __mocks__/ files need `import { vi } from "vitest"` at the top since they are loaded by the test runner.
## feedreader-7dq.28: Standardize fetch mocking
The three incompatible fetch mocking strategies (vi.mock api.js, vi.spyOn globalThis.fetch, window.fetch assignment) are now reduced to two: vi.mock for modules using api(), vi.spyOn for direct fetch callers. window.fetch = vi.fn() is eliminated. vi.stubGlobal('fetch') is also eliminated. Convention documented in srv/static/modules/TESTING.md.

## feedreader-7dq.32: Replace vi.waitFor polling in scraper-page.test.js
flushPromises() (setTimeout-based macrotask flush) is a reliable replacement for vi.waitFor() when all async operations use mocked functions that resolve immediately. Single flush per async step is sufficient. Runtime went from 756ms → 91ms.
## feedreader-7dq.33: Reduce pagination.test.js runtime
Key bottleneck was the PAGE_SIZE test rendering 50 full articles via buildArticleCardHtml + processEmbeds in happy-dom (~300ms). Mocking buildArticleCardHtml to return minimal HTML dropped it to 3ms. The other bottleneck (real 50ms setTimeout) was fixed with vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0). Total: 411ms → 61ms. Approach: mock articles.js via auto-mock at module level, configure buildArticleCardHtml default in beforeEach to return HTML with article-card and article-content-preview classes, then override to minimal HTML in the PAGE_SIZE test.

## feedreader-7dq.34: Consolidate remaining inline vi.mock factories
Created 6 new auto-mocks. Key insight: ESM destructured imports (import { fn } from './mod.js') in source code DO get intercepted by vi.mock() because Vitest makes the module namespace mutable — the import binding is a live reference to the mock. So auto-mocks work correctly for intercepting calls in dependent modules. Two justified inline factories remain: settings-page.test.js (needs importOriginal for real getSetting), views.test.js (stateful store mock). The views.test.js mock could potentially be replaced by using real settings.js with localStorage (same approach as settings-page task .30), but was out of scope.
## feedreader-7dq.29: API response fixture factories
Created 3 factories in test-helpers.js: makeFetchResponse(data, {ok, status}), makeCountsResponse(overrides), makeArticle(overrides). The makeFetchResponse includes both json() and text() methods on the fake Response, which is important since some error paths call .text() instead of .json(). For counts.test.js, a local mockCountsFetch() wrapper that composes both factories eliminated all 13 inline response shapes with a single function call per test.

## feedreader-7dq.31: spa-nav mock reduction
Option A (more integrative tests) was the right call. The module is a genuine orchestrator — it *should* call 6 different modules during navigation. Reducing mocks would mean testing DOM manipulation of real sidebar/views/counts modules, which adds coupling without real benefit. Instead, making each mock earn its assertions revealed that the test file was missing coverage for: popstate handling (reload on non-article pages, restore unread/starred), error handling (both navigateToNavRoute and restoreFromState catch blocks), route filtering (feed/category clicks correctly ignored), and DOM side effects (dropdown clearing, feed-action button hiding). The restoreFromState feed/category paths use dynamic import('./feeds.js') which is harder to test in isolation — would need vi.mock with dynamic import support.

Updated offline.test.js to use mockStandaloneMode() helper (mocks window.matchMedia) instead of vi.spyOn on module export (which doesn't work for ESM internal calls). Added comprehensive tests for standalone code paths: initOfflineSupport (SW ready, offline state, message handler, no-active-worker), handleOnlineStateChange (offline/online transitions with banner, UI state, replay), updateQueueCacheIfStandalone (cache flow, no-active-worker). description:
**Problem:**
`_isStandalone` in `offline.js` is a module-level `const` evaluated once at import time. In happy-dom, `matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)')` always returns `false`, so the value is permanently `false` after import. This makes three functions untestable past their early-return guard:
- `initOfflineSupport()`
- `handleOnlineStateChange()`
- `updateQueueCacheIfStandalone()`

**Fix:**
Convert `_isStandalone` from a constant to a function `isStandalone()`:
```js
// Before
export const _isStandalone = (typeof window.matchMedia === 'function' &&
    window.matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)').matches) ||
    window.navigator.standalone === true;

// After
export function isStandalone() {
    return (typeof window.matchMedia === 'function' &&
        window.matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)').matches) ||
        window.navigator.standalone === true;
}
```

Update the three call sites in `offline.js` to call `isStandalone()` instead of reading the const.

**Tests:**
- Update `offline.test.js` to remove the existing `_isStandalone` constant tests
- Use `vi.spyOn(offlineModule, 'isStandalone').mockReturnValue(true)` to test the standalone code paths in `initOfflineSupport`, `handleOnlineStateChange`, and `updateQueueCacheIfStandalone`
- Cover: SW ready flow, online/offline transitions with banner + UI disable/enable, queue cache updates

**Scope:** 4 lines changed in `offline.js`, plus test additions in `offline.test.js`. No other modules import `_isStandalone`.
title: Refactor _isStandalone from const to function for testability

Key findings: (1) Massive mock duplication: toast.js mocked 13x identically, api.js 7x, plus modal/pagination/feed-errors/counts 3x each. (2) Three incompatible fetch mocking strategies across 17 files. (3) Stateful mock reimplementation in settings-page.test.js. (4) Worst mock-to-test ratio in spa-nav.test.js (6 mocks, 4 tests, 4 assertions). (5) ~100 inline fetch response shapes hardcoded across codebase. (6) Go tests are healthy with minimal mocking. Created 5 follow-up tasks: feedreader-7dq.27 (shared mock helpers), feedreader-7dq.28 (standardize fetch mocking), feedreader-7dq.29 (response fixture factories), feedreader-7dq.30 (replace stateful settings mock), feedreader-7dq.31 (reduce spa-nav mock count).
description:
Review the JS and Go test suites for problems caused by excessive mocking:

1. **Implementation coupling** — Tests that break on refactoring because they assert on internal calls rather than observable behavior. Look for tests where mock setup dominates and assertions are thin.

2. **Mock drift** — Mocks returning data that does not match what the real dependency actually produces (missing fields, wrong types, absent error cases). Compare mock return values against actual API responses and DOM structures.

3. **Suppressed errors** — Mocks on console.error, console.warn, or error handlers that hide real failures (overlaps with feedreader-cnt but broader scope).

4. **Over-isolation** — Places where multiple units are individually mocked but never tested together, meaning integration bugs go undetected.

5. **Fake candidates** — Identify cases where a fake (lightweight real implementation) would be better than a mock:
   - The `api()` module is mocked in nearly every test file. Consider a fake API layer that returns test fixtures and validates request shapes.
   - DOM setup patterns repeated across test files could become shared test utilities.
   - Any dependency mocked with complex conditional logic is a sign it should be a fake instead.

Produce a summary of findings with specific files and patterns. Create follow-up tasks for actionable improvements. Priority for follow-up tasks should be P3.
title: Audit test suite for over-mocking and evaluate fake alternatives

description:
## Problem
13 test files have identical `vi.mock('./toast.js', () => ({ showToast: vi.fn() }))` calls.
7 test files have identical `vi.mock('./api.js', () => ({ api: vi.fn() }))` calls.
modal.js, pagination.js, feed-errors.js, and counts.js are each mocked 3x identically.

This creates maintenance burden — if a module adds an export, all mock sites need updating.

## Solution
Create `srv/static/modules/__test-helpers__/mocks.js` (or similar) with shared mock factory functions. Vitest supports `vi.mock()` with factory functions that can be called from a setup file.

However, note that `vi.mock()` is hoisted to the top of the file by Vitest's transform, so a simple helper function won't work directly. The recommended approach is:

1. Create a `vitest.setup.js` that registers common automocks, OR
2. Use `__mocks__/` directories (Vitest convention) for automatic mocking, OR
3. Create fixture/factory helpers for the *data* (response shapes) while keeping `vi.mock()` calls per-file but with shared mock implementations imported from a helper.

The data factory approach (option 3) is safest and most incremental:
- `makeCountsResponse({unread: 5})` — builds a full counts API response with defaults
- `makeFetchResponse(data)` — wraps data in `{ok: true, json: () => Promise.resolve(data)}`
- `makeArticle({title: 'Test'})` — builds article fixture with defaults

Start with the highest-value targets: toast (13x), api (7x), and the fetch response shape (~100 inline mocks).
title: Create shared test mock helpers to eliminate vi.mock() duplication

Also replaced vi.stubGlobal('fetch', ...) in article-actions.test.js (3) and feeds.test.js (1). Changed all window.fetch assertion references to globalThis.fetch. Added TESTING.md documenting the convention.
description:
## Problem
The test suite uses three incompatible strategies to mock HTTP calls:

1. `vi.mock('./api.js')` — module replacement (7 files: alerts, drag-drop, offline, queue, scraper-page, settings-page, spa-nav)
2. `vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch')` — spy on global (7 files: api, counts, feeds, folders, opml, search, settings)
3. `window.fetch = vi.fn()` — direct assignment (3 files: article-actions, articles, pagination)

### Risk
- Strategy 1 replaces api.js entirely, so changes to api.js error handling/headers are invisible.
- Strategy 2 tests through the real api.js, better for integration but response shape drift is a risk.
- Strategy 3 doesn't auto-restore, can leak between tests, and is the most fragile.

### Mixed strategies within files
article-actions.test.js uses BOTH `window.fetch = vi.fn()` AND `vi.stubGlobal('fetch', ...)` in different tests. feeds.test.js also mixes `vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch')` and `vi.stubGlobal('fetch', ...)`.

## Solution
1. Decide on a standard approach:
   - For modules that import `api()`: mock `./api.js` (strategy 1) — tests the module's logic, not HTTP
   - For `api.test.js` itself: spy on fetch (strategy 2) — tests the real HTTP layer
   - Eliminate strategy 3 (`window.fetch =`) entirely — replace with `vi.spyOn` or `vi.stubGlobal`

2. Replace all `window.fetch = vi.fn()` with `vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch')` which auto-restores in afterEach.

3. Document the convention in a test README or vitest.setup.js comment.

### Files to update
- article-actions.test.js: 20+ `window.fetch = vi.fn()` → `vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch')`
- articles.test.js: 6 `window.fetch = vi.fn()` → `vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch')`
- pagination.test.js: 10 `window.fetch = vi.fn()` → `vi.spyOn(globalThis, 'fetch')`
title: Standardize fetch mocking strategy across JS tests

description:
## Problem
spa-nav.test.js has the worst mock-to-test ratio in the suite:
- 6 vi.mock() calls (api, articles, sidebar, views, feed-errors, counts)
- Only 4 tests with 4 assertions (1.0 expects/test)
- 30 lines of mock setup for 70 lines of actual test code

This suggests either:
1. spa-nav.js has too many responsibilities (orchestrator coupling), or
2. The tests should be more integrative (use fewer mocks, test the integration)

## Solution
Evaluate two approaches:

**Option A: More integrative tests**
- Allow some real modules to execute (e.g., sidebar, views) since they do DOM manipulation that's easy to verify
- Only mock api.js (the network boundary) and counts.js (which also calls the network)
- This would catch integration bugs between spa-nav and its dependencies

**Option B: Refactor spa-nav.js**
- If spa-nav.js is purely a wiring module (connecting navigation events to other modules), it may not need individual unit tests at all
- Instead, test the navigation behavior in an integration-style test that uses real modules

Review spa-nav.js to determine which approach fits better.
title: Reduce mock count in spa-nav.test.js or split spa-nav module

close_reason: Replaced all 23 vi.waitFor() polling calls with flushPromises() (a zero-delay setTimeout promise). Test runtime dropped from 756ms to ~91ms (8.3x improvement). The flushPromises pattern works because all async operations use mocked api() that resolves immediately — a single macrotask flush settles the entire promise chain.
description:
**Problem:**
`scraper-page.test.js` (54 tests, ~740ms) is the slowest test file — ~10x slower than typical. The dominant cause is **23 `vi.waitFor()` calls** using default settings (50ms polling interval, 1000ms timeout). Each poll cycle adds ~50ms of overhead even when the condition resolves immediately. Tests with multiple sequential `vi.waitFor()` calls (e.g., initAiForm success has 3) take 100ms+ each.

**Root cause:**
Functions like `initScraperPage()` and `checkAiStatus()` fire async operations without returning a promise, so tests must poll the DOM to observe side effects.

**Fix approach:**
1. Refactor async init functions to return the promise chain so tests can `await` them directly.
2. Where that is not feasible, use `await flushPromises()` (a zero-delay microtask flush) instead of polling.
3. As a minimal fix, at least set `{ interval: 1 }` on the `vi.waitFor()` calls to reduce poll overhead from 50ms to 1ms per cycle.

**Expected result:**
Test time should drop from ~740ms to under 100ms (in line with similar-sized test files).
title: Replace vi.waitFor polling with direct await in scraper-page.test.js

Also mocked processEmbeds as no-op since no test needed its real behavior.
description:
**Problem:**
`pagination.test.js` (39 tests, ~685ms) is the second slowest test file. Two issues:

1. **Real 50ms sleep** — The `checkScrollForMore` test (line ~460) calls `vi.useRealTimers()` then `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 50))` for a real wall-clock delay. This should use fake timers with `vi.advanceTimersByTime()` instead. (~50ms waste)

2. **50-element DOM rendering in PAGE_SIZE test** — The `does not set done when articles.length equals PAGE_SIZE` test (line ~259) takes ~366ms alone. It generates 50 article cards via `buildArticleCardHtml()`, sets innerHTML, then `processEmbeds()` runs querySelectorAll on each. This is a heavy DOM operation in happy-dom.

**Fix approach:**
1. Replace the real timer with fake timers + `vi.advanceTimersByTime(50)`.
2. For the PAGE_SIZE test, reduce the test data size: instead of creating PAGE_SIZE (50) full article objects, either:
   - Mock `buildArticleCardHtml` to return minimal HTML (the test only checks `.article-card` count and `done` state, not rendering fidelity)
   - Or use a smaller constant and verify the length-vs-PAGE_SIZE comparison logic, not the actual DOM rendering
3. The 15 repeated `Object.defineProperty(window, 'location', ...)` calls per test add minor overhead — consider a shared helper.

**Expected result:**
Test time should drop from ~685ms to under 150ms.
title: Reduce pagination.test.js runtime: real timer delay and heavy DOM test

description:
**Context:**
Commit 0a025ac created `__mocks__/` auto-mock files for the 6 most-mocked modules (toast, api, modal, pagination, counts, feed-errors) and removed 30+ inline factory functions. However, 12 inline `vi.mock()` factory calls remain across 8 test files for 7 additional modules.

**Remaining inline factories (by duplication count):**
- `articles.js` — 3 files (counts, search, spa-nav), each mocking different exports
- `views.js` — 2 files (feeds, spa-nav)
- `settings.js` — 2 files (settings-page, views)
- `offline.js` — 2 files (article-actions, queue), identical factories
- `read-button.js` — 1 file (article-actions)
- `sidebar.js` — 1 file (spa-nav)
- `api.js` — 1 file (spa-nav, needs default resolved promise)

**Fix approach:**
1. Create `__mocks__/` files for modules with 2+ duplicated factories: `articles.js`, `views.js`, `settings.js`, `offline.js`. Each auto-mock should export `vi.fn()` stubs for all commonly-mocked exports from the real module.
2. Replace inline factories with bare `vi.mock()` calls where the auto-mock covers all needed exports.
3. For `spa-nav.test.js`'s api.js mock (needs `api` to return a default resolved promise), keep the inline factory or set the return value in `beforeEach` after using the auto-mock.
4. For single-use factories (`read-button.js`, `sidebar.js`), create auto-mocks only if the export surface is simple; otherwise leave inline.

**Also check for duplicated non-module mocks:**
- `MockIntersectionObserver` is duplicated in `article-actions.test.js` and `articles.test.js` — extract to a shared test utility.

**Verification:** `make check` passes, no test regressions.
title: Consolidate remaining inline vi.mock factories into __mocks__ auto-mocks

close_reason: Pre-grouped feeds by category in getCommonData() as map[int64][]Feed, passed as FeedsByCategory in template data. Changed folder-tree template to use index lookup instead of filtering all feeds per category. Reduces O(feeds×categories) to O(feeds+categories).
description:
The folder-tree template in base.html iterates all 200 feeds for each of 25 categories (O(feeds×categories) = 5,000 reflect-based iterations) to match feeds to folders. This is the dominant cost in page renders — 85% of CPU in profiling — and has pushed / and /category/{id} median response times from ~23ms to ~52ms, consistently failing the 50ms perf threshold.

Fix: build a map[int64][]Feed (categoryID → feeds) in getCommonData() Go code and pass it as FeedsByCategory in template data. Change the folder-tree template to range over (index $root.FeedsByCategory $catID) instead of filtering all feeds per category. This reduces the hot loop from O(feeds×categories) to O(feeds) — a ~25x reduction in reflect.Value.call overhead.

Secondary: consider whether the ETag SHA-256 buffering and the growing getCommonData query set (queue counts, alert counts, user settings) warrant further optimization, but the template loop is the primary bottleneck.
title: Pre-group feeds by category to fix O(N×M) template performance regression

## feedreader-e2d.3.5: Feed source registry
- Created srv/sources/ package with Source interface and Registry

close_reason: Simplified feedName assignment in feeds.js from `((feedType === 'huggingface' || feedType === 'reddit') && !name) ? '' : (name || url)` to `name || ''`. This ensures all feed types send empty name when none is provided, enabling server-side auto-resolution for Steam, Reddit, HuggingFace, and any future feed types.
description:
When adding an RSS feed without typing a name, the client JS sends the URL as the name:

```js
const feedName = ((feedType === 'huggingface' || feedType === 'reddit') && !name) ? '' : (name || url);
```

The `(name || url)` fallback means the server always receives a non-empty name. The server's auto-naming logic (which resolves Steam app names, Reddit subreddit names, etc.) only triggers when `req.Name == ""`, so it's bypassed.

Feed 656 in production shows this: its name is the raw URL `https://store.steampowered.com/news/app/3110760` instead of the resolved game name.

**Fix:** Remove the `feedType` guard so empty names are sent as empty for all feed types, letting the server auto-resolve:

```js
const feedName = name || '';
```

The server already falls back to using the URL as the name when auto-resolution fails, so this is safe for all feed types.

**Files:** `srv/static/modules/feeds.js` (initAddFeedForm)
title: Client sends URL as feed name, preventing server-side Steam name auto-resolution

acceptance_criteria:
1. Pasting a YouTube playlist URL auto-converts to feed URL and fetches successfully
2. Pasting a YouTube channel URL auto-converts to feed URL and fetches successfully
3. Feed auto-names from the YouTube feed title
4. 'Add to top' playlists show new videos via standard RSS polling
5. 'Add to bottom' playlists show new videos via YouTube Data API when API key is configured
6. All YouTube articles display with embedded video player and thumbnail
7. Retention manager does not prematurely delete playlist articles
description: Support YouTube playlists as feed sources. YouTube exposes playlist Atom feeds at `https://www.youtube.com/feeds/videos.xml?playlist_id=PLxxx` but they are limited to 15 entries in playlist-position order. This works for 'add to top' playlists (newest first) but breaks for 'add to bottom' playlists where new items append past position 15 and never appear in the feed. Phase 1 uses the existing RSS/Atom parser with URL auto-detection. Phase 2 adds YouTube Data API v3 integration to support 'add to bottom' playlists via paginated playlistItems.list calls.
design:
Two-phase approach:

**Phase 1 — RSS-only (no API key needed):**
- Auto-convert YouTube URLs to feed URLs in apiCreateFeed: `youtube.com/playlist?list=PLxxx` → `youtube.com/feeds/videos.xml?playlist_id=PLxxx`, and channel URLs → channel feed URLs
- Auto-name feeds from the Atom feed title
- Existing Atom parser already handles YouTube entries (media:group, embeds, thumbnails, yt:video GUIDs)
- This supports 'add to top' playlists immediately

**Phase 2 — YouTube Data API for 'add to bottom' playlists:**
- New user-level setting for YouTube API key (stored in user_settings)
- New feed type `youtube-playlist` that fetches via Data API v3 playlistItems.list
- Supports pagination to reach the last page of a playlist and find newly appended videos
- Track last-seen playlist position or item count to efficiently detect new additions
- Convert API responses to FeedItem structs matching the existing pipeline
- Falls back to RSS if no API key is configured

Articles from both phases are stored and displayed as normal feed items — no playlist-specific UX.
title: YouTube Playlist Feed Support

description:
Add a YouTube Data API v3 client for fetching playlist items. This is needed because the RSS feed only returns the first 15 items in playlist order, which misses new additions to 'add to bottom' playlists.

Implementation:
- New package `srv/youtube/` with a client that calls `playlistItems.list` endpoint
- Accepts an API key (no OAuth needed — public playlist data only)
- Supports pagination via pageToken to traverse the full playlist
- Returns items as FeedItem structs compatible with the existing pipeline
- Extract video title, description, thumbnail, published date, channel/author, and video URL
- Handle API errors gracefully (quota exceeded, invalid key, playlist not found)

The YouTube Data API v3 playlistItems.list endpoint:
- `GET https://www.googleapis.com/youtube/v3/playlistItems?part=snippet&playlistId=PLxxx&maxResults=50&pageToken=xxx`
- Returns up to 50 items per page with nextPageToken for pagination
- snippet includes: title, description, thumbnails, videoOwnerChannelTitle, publishedAt, resourceId.videoId

acceptance_criteria:

description:
The retention manager cleans up articles older than 30 days. For YouTube playlist feeds, this is problematic — a user may add a playlist of old videos they intend to watch over time. The published dates will be far in the past and articles could be cleaned up before the user sees them.

Options (pick one during implementation):
1. Exempt youtube-playlist feed type from retention entirely
2. Add a per-feed 'skip retention' flag  
3. Only retain based on fetched_at (not published_at) for playlist feeds

The simplest approach is option 2 — a per-feed boolean that defaults to true for youtube-playlist feeds. This is also useful for other feed types where users want to keep old articles.
title: Retention exemption for YouTube playlist feeds

description:
Replace the if-then chain in apiCreateFeed with a feed source registry. Currently, URL normalization and auto-naming are scattered across inline if-blocks for Steam, Reddit, and HuggingFace. Adding YouTube would extend this further.

Extract a FeedSource registry where each source declares:
- `Match(url, feedType string) bool` — does this source handle this input?
- `NormalizeURL(ctx, url string) (string, error)` — convert human URL to feed URL (e.g. Steam news → RSS)
- `ResolveName(ctx, url, config string) string` — derive a feed name
- `FeedType() string` — what feed_type to store (e.g. Reddit UI type → "rss" on the wire)

The apiCreateFeed handler iterates the registry instead of branching. Each source is independently testable.

Sources to migrate:
- Steam: NormalizeURL (news→feed conversion) + ResolveName (Steam API name lookup)
- Reddit: ResolveName (regex extract r/name). Note: Reddit URL construction happens client-side, server only auto-names.
- HuggingFace: ResolveName (API call). Note: config-based, feedType stays "huggingface".

This sets up a clean pattern for YouTube and future sources.
title: Refactor feed source handling into a registry pattern

Current indexes are single-column (feed_id, published_at, is_read separately). Add composite indexes that cover the common query patterns: (feed_id, published_at DESC), (is_read, published_at DESC) for the main feed. Generate a weak ETag based on a hash of the rendered output (or a version counter tied to data mutations) and return 304 Not Modified when the client sends a matching If-None-Match. This saves bandwidth and speeds up back/forward navigation. Start with the article detail page (content rarely changes) and the main index.

Three areas of work:

1. **Error paths** — add API failure tests for markRead, markUnread, toggleStar, toggleQueue, and markAsRead. Each should verify console.error and showToast are called.

2. **DOM mutation assertions** — strengthen existing happy-path tests to verify class toggles, aria-labels, innerHTML updates, and updateCounts/updateReadButton calls.

3. **Performance** — replace real-timer sleeps in initQueueState tests with microtask flushing.

notes:
## g2q.1 + g2q.2: Error paths and DOM mutations
- JSDOM normalizes self-closing SVG tags (<path .../> → <path ...></path>), so innerHTML assertions must use toContain with distinguishing path data substrings rather than exact SVG constant matching.
- toast.js mock was added (vi.mock('./toast.js')) to assert showToast calls in error paths.
- The api() wrapper in api.js throws on !res.ok, so error path tests mock fetch to return { ok: false, status: 500, text: ... } which triggers the catch blocks.
- updateCounts, updateQueueCacheIfStandalone, and updateReadButton were already mocked; just needed to import them in the test file to assert they were called.
**Correction**: The test environment is happy-dom (not jsdom as stated above). The self-closing SVG tag normalization behavior is the same in both.
## g2q.3 + g2q.4: markAsRead paths and openArticle assertion
- markAsRead tests share a beforeEach that sets up location with a trackable reload mock and a makeDropdown helper to reduce boilerplate.
- For the reload fallback test, window.location must be an object with a reload function (not just a string) since markAsRead calls both location.href= and location.reload().
- openArticle sets window.location as a string assignment (not .href), so the assertion is `expect(window.location).toBe('/article/5')` — requires Object.defineProperty with writable:true.
## g2q.5 + g2q.6: Delegation tests and microtask flushing
- Delegation tests use `vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0)` after `.click()` to flush async promise chains (markRead/toggleStar/toggleQueue are async).
- toggleQueue delegation test needs its own fetch mock returning `{ queued: true }` since the response shape differs from the default mock.
- The no-article-id guard test verifies that `Number(undefined)` produces NaN which is falsy, so the early return fires correctly.
- `vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0)` is sufficient to flush the microtask queue for initQueueState's `.then()` chain — no need for real timers or sleep.

close_reason: All 13 child tasks completed. Every init*Listeners function across all JS modules now uses AbortController for signal-based listener cleanup. Pattern: module-level AC variable, abort/recreate at function entry, { signal } on all addEventListener calls. Modules with _reset*State functions also abort there. Tests updated for scraper-page and offline modules.
description:
Multiple modules register document/window-level event listeners in init functions without cleanup. If called multiple times, listeners accumulate and handlers fire multiple times. This was already fixed in article-actions.js using AbortController signals. The same pattern should be applied to all other modules with init functions that register document-level listeners.

Currently a test-only concern (each init runs once per page load in production), but will become a real bug if the app moves further toward SPA navigation.
design:
For each affected module:
1. Add a module-level `let _listenerAC = null` AbortController variable
2. At the top of each init function, abort previous and create new: `if (_listenerAC) _listenerAC.abort(); _listenerAC = new AbortController(); const signal = _listenerAC.signal;`
3. Pass `{ signal }` (or `{ capture: true, signal }` for capture listeners) to each addEventListener call
4. Add cleanup in any existing reset/teardown function
5. Update tests if needed

Reference implementation: article-actions.js initArticleActionListeners()
title: Add AbortController cleanup to all init*Listeners functions

close_reason: Moved import map script tag before modulepreload link tags in base.html. This ensures the import map is processed before any module loading begins, fixing ~15 browser console warnings about conflicting import map rules.
description:
**Problem:**
Browser console shows ~15 warnings on every page load:
`An import map rule for specifier 'https://lynx-fairy.exe.xyz/static/modules/X.js' was removed, as it conflicted with already resolved module specifiers.`

**Root Cause:**
In `base.html`, the `{{modulePreloadTags}}` output (which generates `<link rel="modulepreload">` elements) appears BEFORE the `<script type="importmap">` tag. Per the HTML spec, import maps must be processed before any module loading begins. When the browser encounters modulepreload links first, it starts fetching and resolving those modules. By the time it reaches the import map, those module specifiers are already resolved, so the import map rules for those specifiers are discarded.

The import map exists to remap bare module paths (e.g., `/static/modules/api.js`) to versioned URLs (`/static/modules/api.js?v=hash`) for cache busting. Modules import each other using relative paths (`./api.js`) which resolve to the unversioned URLs. Without the import map being active, these inter-module imports bypass cache busting.

**Introduced in:**
- Phase 4 (commit 98218de): Added import map
- Phase 6 (commit cd8c931): Added modulepreload hints — placed BEFORE the import map

**Fix:**
Move the import map `<script>` tag before the modulepreload `<link>` tags in `base.html`. The correct order is:
1. `<script type="importmap">` — must come first
2. `<link rel="modulepreload">` — can hint at versioned URLs
3. `<script type="module">` — entry point

The modulepreload hrefs already use versioned URLs (`?v=hash`), so they will still work correctly as cache-busted preload hints. The import map will be active when the entry point script triggers module resolution via relative paths.

**Verification:**
- No console warnings about conflicting import map rules
- Network tab shows versioned URLs for all module requests
- Cache busting still works (change a module, reload, new version fetched)
title: Fix import map vs modulepreload ordering in base.html

description: feeds.test.js has 40 tests covering 8 of 11 exported functions. Three functions lack direct tests (editFeed, createEditFeedModal, closeEditModal delegate handling), several tested functions have untested branches, and 6 initAddFeedForm tests each sleep 50ms real time, making them 74% of total test duration (322ms of 435ms).
design:
Two areas of work:

1. **Coverage gaps** — add tests for untested exports and untested branches in existing tests. Each gap is a separate task so they can be worked independently.

2. **Performance** — replace `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 50))` in initAddFeedForm tests with microtask flushing or fake timers to eliminate 322ms of wall-clock sleep.
notes:
## r4e.1 & r4e.2: editFeed/createEditFeedModal and refreshFeed tests
- createEditFeedModal appends to document.body, so no DOM setup needed in beforeEach — but the modal persists across tests since beforeEach clears body.innerHTML
- editFeed internally calls createEditFeedModal, so testing editFeed also implicitly tests modal creation
- refreshFeed fake timer tests need careful sequencing: await promise first, then advance timers for the restore setTimeout. The pattern is: call function -> advance 1s for polling -> await promise -> assert intermediate state -> advance 2s for restore -> assert final state
- The catch path test is simpler because the error is synchronous (fetch rejects immediately), no polling loop to advance through
## r4e.5 & r4e.6: Side-effect assertions and setTimeout elimination
- Mocking views.js and feed-errors.js at file level works cleanly since feeds.js imports them directly. Pattern: vi.mock('./views.js', () => ({ applyDefaultViewForScope: vi.fn() }))
- vi.waitFor with { interval: 1 } is the right replacement for setTimeout sleeps when waiting for async event handlers. Default 50ms interval defeats the purpose.
- The initAddFeedForm submit handler is async but attached via addEventListener, so we can't await it directly. vi.waitFor polling on the terminal side-effect (reloadMock or showToast) is the clean pattern.
- showFeedErrorBanner test was previously checking DOM output; now checks the mock call since feed-errors.js is mocked. This is more precise and doesn't depend on feed-errors.js implementation details.
## r4e.3 & r4e.4: saveFeed sidebar/header and initFeedActionListeners delegations
- saveFeed sidebar test: append a sidebar element with .feed-item[href="/feed/{id}"] .feed-name to the existing beforeEach DOM; the function uses an attribute selector on href so the exact format matters.
- saveFeed header/title test: requires Object.defineProperty to mock window.location.pathname since happy-dom doesn't support direct assignment. The pathname comparison in saveFeed is strict equality with the template literal.
- initFeedActionListeners close-edit-modal: straightforward click delegation test, just need the modal with display:flex and a child with data-action.
- initFeedActionListeners form-submit: uses Event('submit', { bubbles: true }) since the delegation is on document. The form needs all edit-feed-* inputs to avoid null reference errors in saveFeed.
title: Improve feeds.test.js coverage and performance

description:
Six initAddFeedForm tests each use `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 50))` to wait for the async submit handler. This costs 50ms wall-clock per test — 322ms total, 74% of the file's runtime.

Fix: replace the sleep pattern with either:
- `vi.useFakeTimers()` + `vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(0)`
- Direct promise chain awaiting (dispatch event, await flush)
- `await vi.waitFor(() => expect(...))`

design:
# ES Modules Refactor Plan

Refactor `srv/static/app.js` (2277 lines, plain `<script>`) into ES modules.

## Goals

1. **Coverage** — V8 coverage works with real ES module imports
2. **Testability** — direct imports replace the `new Function()` eval hack
3. **Maintainability** — split monolith into focused, navigable modules
4. **Tooling** — static analysis, go-to-definition, dead code detection

## Current State (after Phase 4)

- `app.js` is a 363-line entry point loaded as `<script type="module">`
- 20 ES modules in `srv/static/modules/`, each with a companion `.test.js`
- 390 tests across 20 test files, all using direct ES module imports
- Zero inline event handlers — all use `data-action` + `addEventListener`
- Zero `window.X` global exports
- Import maps provide cache busting for all module files
- 82% statement / 85% function coverage on modules
- 1 `setSidebarLoadCategory()` late-binding call remains (legitimate top-down wiring)
- 2 shared leaf modules (`feed-errors.js`, `read-button.js`) break former cycles
- 1 safe circular ES module import (`articles ↔ pagination`) with hoisted functions

## Proposed Module Structure

```
srv/static/
  app.js              → thin entry point (imports modules, runs init)
  modules/
    api.js            → api() fetch wrapper
    settings.js       → getSetting, saveSetting, applyUserPreferences,
                        applyHideReadArticles, applyHideEmptyFeeds
    utils.js          → formatTimeAgo, formatLocalDate, stripHtml,
                        truncateText, getArticleSortTime
    icons.js          → SVG constant strings
    articles.js       → renderArticleActions, buildArticleCardHtml,
                        renderArticles, updateReadButton,
                        showArticlesLoading, updateAllReadMessage,
                        showReadArticles, showHiddenArticles,
                        processEmbeds, extractYouTubeId
    article-actions.js→ markRead, markUnread, toggleStar, toggleQueue,
                        markReadSilent, openArticle, openArticleExternal,
                        markAsRead, auto-mark-read observer
    pagination.js     → cursor state, loadMoreArticles, checkScrollForMore,
                        updatePaginationCursor, updateEndOfArticlesIndicator
    sidebar.js        → toggleSidebar, setSidebarActive, navigateFolder,
                        toggleFolderCollapse, collapseFolder
    feeds.js          → loadFeedArticles, loadCategoryArticles,
                        refreshFeed, deleteFeed, editFeed, saveFeed,
                        filterFeeds, setFeedCategory, feed error banner,
                        createEditFeedModal, closeEditModal
    folders.js        → openCreateFolderModal, closeCreateFolderModal,
                        submitCreateFolder, renameCategory,
                        unparentCategory, deleteCategory
    counts.js         → updateCounts, updateFeedStatusCell,
                        updateFeedErrors
    views.js          → setView, getViewScope, initView,
                        migrateLegacyViewDefaults, getDefaultViewForScope,
                        applyDefaultViewForScope
    drag-drop.js      → initDragDrop, initFolderDragDrop,
                        syncFolderOrder, reorderElements,
                        getDragAfterElementAmongSiblings
    opml.js           → exportOPML, importOPML
    queue.js          → queuedArticleIds, queuedIdsReady, queueNext
    settings-page.js  → initSettingsPage, runCleanup, newsletter functions
    offline.js        → service worker, online/offline handling,
                        cacheQueueForOffline, replayPendingActions
    dropdown.js       → toggleDropdown, click-outside listener
    timestamps.js     → initTimestampTooltips
    scraper-page.js   → scraper tab switching, schema panel, config modal
                        (currently inline in scrapers.html <script>)
```

## Migration Strategy

### Phase 1: Infrastructure (non-breaking)

1. Create `modules/` directory
2. Extract pure utility modules first (`utils.js`, `icons.js`, `api.js`)
   — these have zero DOM dependencies and are easiest to test
3. Update `vitest.config.mjs` to include `modules/` in coverage
4. Write module-style tests that directly import from the new files
5. Verify old tests still pass (both test styles coexist temporarily)

### Phase 2: Extract stateful modules

6. Extract `settings.js`, `views.js`, `dropdown.js`, `timestamps.js`
7. Extract `articles.js`, `article-actions.js`, `pagination.js`
8. Extract `sidebar.js`, `feeds.js`, `folders.js`, `counts.js`
9. Extract `drag-drop.js`, `opml.js`, `queue.js`
10. Extract `settings-page.js`, `offline.js`

During this phase, `app.js` gradually shrinks as code moves out. Each
extracted module exports its public functions. The entry point imports
and either calls them or registers them.

### Phase 3: Eliminate inline handlers

11. Replace `onclick="fn()"` in templates with `data-action` attributes
    or stable selectors, and wire them up via `addEventListener` in the
    entry point or per-page init functions
12. Replace `onclick` strings built in JS (article cards, modals) with
    DOM creation + `addEventListener`
13. Remove the ESLint `varsIgnorePattern` whitelist
14. Remove all `window.X = X` assignments (nothing needs to be global)

### Phase 4: Finalize

15. Change `<script src="/static/app.js">` to
    `<script type="module" src="/static/app.js">` in `base.html`
16. Delete `test-helper.js` and the eval-based test machinery
17. Rewrite tests as direct ES module imports
18. Verify coverage reports meaningful numbers
19. Update `AGENTS.md` code layout docs

### Phase 5: Eliminate late-bound dependencies

The `setXxxDeps()` pattern was used during Phase 2 to break circular
import dependencies. Of the 6 uses, only 3 are actual cycles — the
other 5 are unnecessary and can be replaced with direct imports. The
3 real cycles can be eliminated through small restructurings.

**Non-circular (replace with direct imports):**

- `article-actions` → `counts.updateCounts` — no cycle
- `article-actions` → `offline.updateQueueCacheIfStandalone` — no cycle
- `counts` → `articles.applyUserPreferences` — no cycle
- `offline` → `counts.updateCounts` — no cycle
- `queue` → `offline.updateQueueCacheIfStandalone` — no cycle

**Real cycles (restructure to eliminate):**

20. `article-actions ↔ articles`: `article-actions` needs
    `updateReadButton` from `articles`, and `articles` imports
    `queuedArticleIds`, `queuedIdsReady`, `initAutoMarkRead` from
    `article-actions`. Fix: extract `updateReadButton` into a small
    shared module (e.g. `read-button.js`) — it's a pure DOM
    manipulation function that updates a button's icon and data
    attributes. Both modules import from it.

21. `articles ↔ pagination`: `pagination` imports
    `buildArticleCardHtml`, `processEmbeds`, `applyUserPreferences`,
    `getShowingHiddenArticles` from `articles`, while `articles`
    needs `updatePaginationCursor`, `updateEndOfArticlesIndicator`,
    `setPaginationState` from `pagination`. Fix: the pagination state
    functions that `articles` needs are really part of "rendering
    articles" — move them into `articles.js`, making `pagination.js`
    a pure "load more" module that calls into `articles`.
    Alternatively, extract the shared rendering functions
    (`buildArticleCardHtml`, `processEmbeds`) into a lower-level
    `article-rendering.js`.

22. `counts ↔ feeds` and `sidebar ↔ feeds`: `feeds` imports
    `updateCounts` from `counts` and `setSidebarActive` from
    `sidebar`. `counts` needs `showFeedErrorBanner` /
    `removeFeedErrorBanner` from `feeds`. `sidebar` needs
    `loadCategoryArticles` from `feeds`. Fix: move
    `showFeedErrorBanner`/`removeFeedErrorBanner` into `counts.js`
    (or a new `feed-errors.js`) since they're only called from
    `updateFeedErrors` in counts. For sidebar → feeds,
    `loadCategoryArticles` is a high-level orchestration function —
    keep the callback from `app.js` (this is legitimate top-down
    wiring, not a circular dependency hack).

After this phase, the only remaining `setXxxDeps` call should be
`setSidebarLoadCategory` in `app.js`, which is genuine top-down
wiring from the entry point.

### Phase 6: Clean up app.js entry point

`app.js` still contains ~160 lines of page-specific logic that
belongs in modules. The entry point should only import, wire
dependencies, and call init functions.

**Move to existing modules:**

23. Add feed form handler (72 lines) → `feeds.js` as
    `initAddFeedForm()`. Contains Reddit URL building, HuggingFace
    config construction, category assignment — all feed-specific
    logic with no entry-point concerns.

24. Feed item click handler (12 lines) → `feeds.js` as part of
    `initFeedActionListeners()`. SPA feed navigation that already
    calls `loadFeedArticles`.

25. Sidebar mobile close (8 lines) → `sidebar.js` as part of
    `initSidebarListeners()`. Sidebar behavior.

26. Pagination bootstrap + scroll listener (11 lines) →
    `pagination.js` as `initPagination()`. Reads DOM for initial
    cursor state and registers the scroll listener — pure pagination
    concerns.

27. Queue hydration (16 lines) → `article-actions.js` as
    `initQueueState()`. Fetches queue IDs from the API, populates
    `queuedArticleIds`, and renders action-button placeholders.
    Already owns that state.

28. Drag prevention (4 lines) → `drag-drop.js` as part of its init.

**Move to new module:**

29. Search handler (36 lines) → new `search.js` module. Self-contained
    feature with its own state (`originalHTML`, `searchAbort`,
    debounce timer) and no dependency on other app.js code.

**Keep in app.js:**

- Imports (~25 lines after cleanup)
- Listener init calls (~16 lines)
- Expand active folder parents (8 lines) — cross-cutting DOM init
- One-liner init calls: `initView`, `initFolderDragDrop`, page inits
- `setInterval(updateCounts, 60000)` — app-level polling policy
- `setSidebarLoadCategory` wiring (legitimate top-down callback)

After this phase, `app.js` should be ~120 lines — almost entirely
imports, init calls, and DOMContentLoaded sequencing.

Phase 6 also adds `<link rel="modulepreload">` hints in `base.html`
for all 20 modules. The ES module import waterfall added +328ms to
cold DOMContentLoaded (the browser discovers imports sequentially
across 3 depth levels). Modulepreload lets the browser fetch all
modules in parallel upfront, eliminating the waterfall.

## Key Decisions

### No bundler

Modern browsers handle ES module imports natively. This is a single-user
app behind a proxy — the extra HTTP requests for ~15 module files are
irrelevant. A bundler adds build complexity for no real benefit here.

### Transitional `window` exports

During Phases 2–3, functions still called from `onclick` attributes must
remain on `window`. The entry point can do:
```js
import { toggleStar } from './modules/article-actions.js';
window.toggleStar = toggleStar;
```
This is removed in Phase 3 when inline handlers are eliminated.

### Shared state

Mutable state (`paginationCursorTime`, `queuedArticleIds`, etc.) lives
in the module that owns it, exposed via getter/setter functions rather
than direct variable access. This makes dependencies explicit.

### Test migration

Tests can be migrated incrementally. As each module is extracted, its
tests switch from `window.fn()` to `import { fn }`. The eval-based
test helper remains functional for not-yet-extracted code.

## Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|------------|
| Circular dependencies between modules | 3 real cycles identified (see Phase 5); resolved via small restructurings — extract shared leaf modules or consolidate tightly-coupled state |
| Breaking inline handlers mid-migration | Transitional `window` exports keep everything working |
| `<script type="module">` is deferred (runs later than classic scripts) | This app already uses `DOMContentLoaded`; deferred execution is equivalent |
| `type="module"` doesn't run in very old browsers | Not a concern — single-user app on modern browser |
| Large diff is hard to review | Phase-by-phase commits, each independently passing `make check` |

Plus ~20 inline onclick strings built in JS modules (articles.js, feeds.js).
- Replaced 4 inline handlers in `base.html` with `data-action` attributes: `toggle-sidebar` (menu button + overlay), `toggle-folder` (chevron), `navigate-folder` (folder link). Added `initSidebarListeners()` to sidebar.js using delegated event listeners on document. Chevron uses capture phase so `stopPropagation()` works correctly.
- Replaced 2 inline handlers in `category_settings.html`: `unparentCategory` button uses `data-action="unparent-category"`, `deleteExclusion` button uses `data-action="delete-exclusion"`. **Key patterns established for Phase 3:**
- Template inline handlers → `data-action="action-name"` + `data-*` attributes for parameters
- Delegated listeners via `document.addEventListener('click', ...)` matching `[data-action]`

## Notes

- Measured via browser Performance API through mitmdump reverse proxy
  (localhost:3000 → localhost:8000)
- 33 of 35 requests are sidebar favicon images (~1 KB each, 82–224ms each
  due to DB lookups / proxying)
- One fetch request to `/api/queue` (120 KB, 92ms) fires after DOMContentLoaded
- JS/CSS load times are negligible (<20ms) since it's all localhost
- After the refactor, expect ~15 JS module requests replacing the single

  be minimal given localhost latency.

## How to Re-measure

```bash
# Start auth proxy if not running
mitmdump --mode reverse:http://localhost:8000 --listen-port 3000 \
  --set modify_headers='/~q/X-Exedev-Email/test@example.com' \

```

Then in the browser console on `http://localhost:3000/`:

```js
const nav = performance.getEntriesByType('navigation')[0];
const paint = performance.getEntriesByType('paint');
const resources = performance.getEntriesByType('resource');
console.table({
  ttfb: Math.round(nav.responseStart),
  domInteractive: Math.round(nav.domInteractive),
  domContentLoaded: Math.round(nav.domContentLoadedEventEnd),
  loadComplete: Math.round(nav.loadEventEnd),
});
console.log('Paint:', paint.map(p => `${p.name}: ${Math.round(p.startTime)}ms`));
console.log(`${resources.length} requests, ${Math.round(resources.reduce((s,r) => s + (r.transferSize||0), 0) / 1024)} KB`);
```

# Page Load Benchmark — After ES Modules Refactor

Captured **2026-02-25** after completing Phases 1–4 of the ES modules
refactor. `app.js` is now a 363-line entry point loading 20 ES modules
via `<script type="module">` with import maps for cache busting.

## Navigation Timing (cold load)

| Metric               | Time   | Δ from baseline |
|----------------------|--------|----------------|
| TTFB                 | 257ms  | +133ms         |
| DOM Interactive      | 378ms  | +160ms         |
| DOMContentLoaded     | 554ms  | +328ms         |
| Load Complete        | 570ms  | +339ms         |
| First Paint          | 404ms  | +168ms         |
| First Contentful Paint | 404ms | +168ms         |

## Navigation Timing (warm/cached load)

| Metric               | Time       |
|----------------------|------------|
| TTFB                 | 223–264ms  |
| DOMContentLoaded     | 287–331ms  |
| Load Complete        | 293–336ms  |
| First Paint          | 292–336ms  |
| Transfer             | **0 KB**   |

## JS/CSS Assets

| Asset                  | Raw Size | Transfer (gzip) |
|------------------------|----------|----------------|
| app.js                 | 13 KB    | 4 KB           |
| modules/feeds.js       | 17 KB    | 4 KB           |
| modules/articles.js    | 13 KB    | 4 KB           |
| modules/article-actions.js | 12 KB | 3 KB          |
| modules/scraper-page.js | 11 KB   | 3 KB           |
| modules/drag-drop.js   | 9 KB     | 2 KB           |
| modules/offline.js     | 8 KB     | 3 KB           |
| 14 smaller modules     | 36 KB    | 12 KB          |
| **Total JS**           | **119 KB** | **35 KB**    |
| style.css              | 52 KB    | 13 KB          |

## Full Page Summary

| Metric           | Value          | Δ from baseline |
|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| Total requests   | 58             | +23            |
| JS requests      | 21             | +20            |
| JS raw size      | 119 KB         | +32 KB (+37%)  |
| JS gzip size     | 35 KB          | +9 KB (+35%)   |
| Favicon requests | 35             | +2             |

## Analysis

**Cold load is slower** due to the ES module import waterfall. The
browser discovers imports sequentially as it parses each module,
creating a dependency chain. With 20 modules and a 3-level import
depth, this adds multiple sequential round trips even on localhost.

**Warm load is fast** — import maps with content hashes enable
immutable caching (`max-age=31536000`), so subsequent visits transfer
0 bytes and load in ~300ms, comparable to the pre-refactor cold load.

**Raw JS size grew 37%** — import/export statements and late-binding
boilerplate (`setXxxDeps` patterns) add overhead. Gzip is also less
effective across many small files vs. one large file.

## Potential Optimizations

- **`<link rel="modulepreload">`** for each module in `base.html`
  would eliminate the waterfall by fetching all modules in parallel
- **Phase 5/6 cleanup** will reduce module count and remove
  late-binding boilerplate
- **Bundling** for production remains an option but was explicitly
  deferred (see ES_MODULES_PLAN.md)

close_reason: Added spa-nav.js module for SPA sidebar navigation. Nav-item clicks for / and /starred now swap content via AJAX. Added popstate handler for back/forward. Added /api/articles/starred endpoint. Updated pagination, sidebar, and feeds modules to work across all article-list pages.
description:
Sidebar navigation is only partially SPA-ified. After the ES modules refactor, clicking feed-items and folder-items on the main articles page use pushState + fetch to swap content without a full reload. However, the rest of the sidebar still causes full page reloads:

1. **Top-level nav items** (All Articles, Starred, Queue, History, Alerts, Scrapers, Settings, Feeds) are plain `<a href>` links with no SPA interception — they always trigger a full page reload.

2. **No popstate handler** — pressing browser back/forward after SPA-navigating to a feed or folder reloads the entire page instead of restoring content via AJAX.

3. **Feed/folder SPA clicks are page-gated** — `initFeedItemClickListeners()` checks for `.articles-view` and falls through to normal navigation if absent. So clicking a feed while on `/starred` or `/history` causes a full reload.

The expected behavior is that clicking any sidebar element on an article-list page (/, /starred, /feed/N, /category/N) updates only the content area via AJAX, preserving scroll state, sidebar collapse state, and avoiding a full document reload.

Key files:
- `srv/static/modules/sidebar.js` — folder SPA navigation
- `srv/static/modules/feeds.js` — `loadFeedArticles()`, `loadCategoryArticles()`, `initFeedItemClickListeners()`
- `srv/static/app.js` — init wiring
- `srv/templates/base.html` — sidebar markup (nav-items, feed-items, folder-links)

To fix:
- Add SPA click interception for nav-items (/, /starred) that share the article-list layout
- Add a `popstate` event handler to restore content when back/forward is pressed
- Ensure feed/folder clicks SPA-navigate even from non-index article-list pages
- Pages with entirely different layouts (settings, feeds management, scrapers) can remain full-nav
